coredata.dc:title: A nanohybrid magnetic sensing probe for levofloxacin determination integrates porous graphene, selective polymer and graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Chansud, Nutnicha
Longnapa, Noppanut
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A nanohybrid magnetic fluorescent sensing probe was designed and fabricated for ultrasensitive and selective determination of levofloxacin. The probe integrated porous graphene (PGr), magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into selective molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The developed probe was sensitive, selective, and its binding ability enriched levofloxacin in complex samples. The fabrication strategy was evaluated to achieve the best performance and the synthesized sensing probe was characterized. In the best condition, the fluorescence emission of the probe was quenched linearly from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 of levofloxacin and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg L-1. The quenching of fluorescence was not affected by the analog compounds ciprofloxacin, lomefloxacin, marbofloxacin and sarafloxacin. The imprinting factor of the developed nanohybrid sensing probe was 4.26. The developed probe was utilized to detect levofloxacin in milk and recoveries between 91.8 % and 100.5 % were achieved with RSDs <6.5 %. Analysis with the optosensor provided the same results as HPLC analysis but the optosensor was more sensitive, less expensive, simpler and more rapid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous graphene
Optosensor
Nanoprobe
Graphene quantum dots
MIP
Levofloxacin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe
Characterization of related materials and nanohybrid sensing
Ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand ( NRCT5-RSA63022-03 ), the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC) , Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation . The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The strategy for the synthesis of the hybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe (A) and interaction between levofloxacin and specific recognition sites of the probe (B).

SEM images are of porous graphene (A) and the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe; the inset shows a particle size distribution histogram (B). FT-IR spectra are of Fe3O4@SiO2 (C), porous graphene (D), graphene quantum dots (E) and PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe (F).

The emission spectra (A) are of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe bound with levofloxacin template (a), PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe after removal of template (levofloxacin) (b) and the PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe (c). The photograph (B) shows the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe with (left) and without (right) template.

Effects of incubation time (A), ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker (B), pH of dispersion solution (C) and amount of porous graphene (D) on the detection of levofloxacin by the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe.

Fluorescence emission spectra are of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe (A) and the PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe (B) in the presence of different concentrations of levofloxacin. The calibration curves (C) are for the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 and PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe. The chart (D) shows the sensitivity toward levofloxacin of different composite nanoprobes.

The charts show the selectivity of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe for levofloxacin detection (A), a competitive study (B), fabrication reproducibility (C) and stability of the hybrid sensing probe in buffer solution at pH 6.0 (D).

The chromatogram (A) is of an original milk sample (a), and milk samples spiked at 2.0 (b), 5.0 (c), 10.0 (d) and 20.0 mg L−1 (e) of levofloxacin. The plots (B) show the similarity of the results obtained from the optosensor and HPLC for the determination of levofloxacin.
The determination of levofloxacin in milk using the optosensor based on a nanohybrid magnetic PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe.
The determination of levofloxacin in various samples, and a comparison between the developed optosensor and other methods.

head.ce:title: A nanohybrid magnetic sensing probe for levofloxacin determination integrates porous graphene, selective polymer and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla, 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nanohybrid magnetic fluorescent sensing probe was designed and fabricated for ultrasensitive and selective determination of levofloxacin. The probe integrated porous graphene (PGr), magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into selective molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The developed probe was sensitive, selective, and its binding ability enriched levofloxacin in complex samples. The fabrication strategy was evaluated to achieve the best performance and the synthesized sensing probe was characterized. In the best condition, the fluorescence emission of the probe was quenched linearly from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 of levofloxacin and the limit of detection was 0.03 μg L-1. The quenching of fluorescence was not affected by the analog compounds ciprofloxacin, lomefloxacin, marbofloxacin and sarafloxacin. The imprinting factor of the developed nanohybrid sensing probe was 4.26. The developed probe was utilized to detect levofloxacin in milk and recoveries between 91.8 % and 100.5 % were achieved with RSDs <6.5 %. Analysis with the optosensor provided the same results as HPLC analysis but the optosensor was more sensitive, less expensive, simpler and more rapid.

ce:section.ce:para: Levofloxacin is a third-generation fluoroquinolones antibiotic which is widely applied in human and animal patients for the treatment of bacterial infections, pneumonia and immunodeficiency virus. However, the overuse of levofloxacin not only causes antimicrobial resistance but leaves drug residues which adversely affect human health [1]. Although, there is no maximum residue level (MRL) of levofloxacin in foods, however, it is necessary to accurately and reliably determine levofloxacin to protect human health. The compound can be detected by electrochemical sensor [1,2], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], voltammetry [4], capillary zone electrophoresis [5], UV spectrophotometry [6], chemiluminescence [7] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [8]. HPLC is the most widely used for the detection of levofloxacin due to its accuracy and precision [9]. However, HPLC involves expensive instrument, complicated procedures and requires large volumes of mobile phase, normally made up of toxic organic solvents. To avoid these problems, an optosensor based on a nanoprobe is an interesting alternative method. This approach involves cost-effective equipment, simple procedures and does not require toxic organic solvents. In addition, the sensitivity and selectivity can be improved using a composite probe that integrates good optical materials and highly specific materials [10,11]. Quantum dots (QDs) are a good sensing material due to their relatively high quantum yields, narrow emission and good photostability [12]. Various types of QDs have been utilized for sensing applications, including inorganic semiconductor QDs [13], and carbon-based QDs [14]. Since inorganic semiconductor based QDs contain highly toxic heavy metals, the environmentally friendly carbon-based QDs are more appropriate [15]. Graphene QDs (GQDs) have demonstrated their low toxicity, photo-stability and hydrophilicity [16]. In an effort to prevent the agglomeration of QDs and to improve the selectivity of the sensing probe, QDs nanoparticles can be incorporated into appropriate matrices. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) is a useful material for the fabrication of selective hybrid sensing probes since it can carry three-dimensional specific recognition sites [17]. In the imprinting process, the polymerization of a monomer and a cross-linker in the presence of the target molecule (template) forms an imprinted polymer. Removal of the template leaves complementary binding cavities in the polymer [18,19], resulting in highly selective recognition cavities that can receive only the specific target molecule [20]. Recently, fluorescence sensing probes using MIP composited or coated on QDs have engaged interest for the detection of several target analytes at trace levels such as tetracycline [21], paclitaxel [22], naproxen [23], chloramphenicol [24]. In addition, the mass transfer of the analyte to the recognition sites can be improved by the inclusion of a highly adsorptive material in combination with GQDs and MIP [25]. For the adsorption of levofloxacin, porous graphene (PGr) is a good candidate material since it can adsorb levofloxacin via hydrogen bonding and π-π interaction. However, residual concentrations of levofloxacin are normally low and food samples usually contain various matrix interferences. To facilitate the detection of ultra-trace analyte levels, pre-concentration of the analyte with a highly sensitive and selective probe is crucial. The pre-concentration, or enrichment, of target analytes can be performed by incorporating magnetite nanoparticles in the hybrid sensing probe.

In this work, a nanohybrid sensing probe was designed and fabricated for the first time for the determination of levofloxacin. The nanohybrid sensing probe integrated a good adsorptive property of PGr, good optical property of GQDs, high selectivity of MIP material and utilized Fe3O4 nanoparticles for enrichment of levofloxacin (PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2). The detection of levofloxacin was based on the fluorescence quenching of the probe when levofloxacin bound to the recognition sites of the probe. A reliable, simple, rapid, low-cost, sensitive and stable optosensor was fabricated for the determination of levofloxacin. The optosensor was utilized to determine levofloxacin in food and the performance of the proposed method was compared with a conventional HPLC method.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, ≥99 %) and levofloxacin were from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98 %), citric acid, FeCl2.4H2O and FeCl3.6H2O were from Sigma-Aldrich (MO, USA). Ammonium (NH4OH), ethanol and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were from RCI Labscan (Bangkok, Thailand). Porous graphene (PGr) (C2131121D3) was from Gwent Group Ltd., (Mamhilad, Pontypool, UK). Di-sodium hydrogen orthophosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4⋅12H2O) was purchased from Univar (Ajax finechem, Australia). Dialysis bags were from Spectrum Laboratories, Inc. (CA, USA).
Details of the instruments are provided in the Supplementary Information.
Magnetite nanoparticles were synthesized by modification of a previous method [26] and the detail is provided in the Supplementary Information.

GQDs were prepared via pyrolysis according to the following procedure [14] in which 2.00 g of citric acid were first heated at 250 °C until the liquid became yellow. In a beaker, 1.0 g of NaOH was dissolved in 98.5 mL of DI water and 1.50 mL of APTES were added with stirring at 500 rpm. The yellow liquid citric acid was added dropwise into the mixture solution and continuously stirred for 30 min to obtain the synthesized GQDs, which were purified in a dialysis bag under gentle stirring for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: The nanohybrid composite PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe was fabricated as demonstrated in Fig. 1 A. Firstly, 1.50 mL of APTES were introduced into a brown bottle containing 20 mL of GQDs solution under gentle stirring and heated at 40 °C for 30 min to coat the GQDs with APTES. To synthesize the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe, 9.0 mg of levofloxacin as the template molecule and 48 μL of APTES monomer were dissolved into 5.0 mL of DI water and stirred at 500 rpm for 1 h at room temperature. Then, 5.0 mL of the APTES-coated GQDs were added into the mixture solution and stirred for 1 h. To complete the synthesis, 404 μL of TEOS cross-linker, 150 μL of NH4OH catalyst, 5.0 mg of Fe3O4@SiO2 and 10.0 mg of PGr were added and the mixture solution was continuously stirred for 4 h. The synthesized PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanohybrid sensing probes were washed in 20 mL of ethanol under stirring for 30 min to remove the template. The interaction between levofloxacin and the specific recognition sites created on the hybrid sensing probe is proposed in Fig. 1B. Nanohybrid non-imprinted polymer (NIP) probes were also synthesized under the same conditions without the addition of levofloxacin.

The hybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe and PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe were separately dispersed in citrate-phosphate buﬀ ;er solution at pH 6.0. Then, 300 μL of the dispersions were mixed with 2.0 mL of pretreated milk sample or levofloxacin standards at different concentrations and incubated for 12 min. The nanohybrid sensing probe was separated using a magnet and dispersed in 100 μL of citrate-phosphate buffer at pH 6.0. Setting an excitation wavelength at 355 nm, emissions were measured and recorded from 380 to 630 nm with a slit width of 10 nm. The determination was performed at 25 °C for convenience.

The analysis of levofloxacin using HPLC was performed on an Agilent 1100 series HPLC system (Germany). The separation column was a Fortis C18 (4.6 mm ×15 cm, 5 μm) and the mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile and 0.10 M acetic acid (25:75 v/v) at a constant flow rate of 1.00 mL min−1. The sample injection volume and column temperature were 20 μL and 30 °C, respectively. The detection of levofloxacin was performed at 254 nm.
The detail of sample pretreatment is provided in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of related materials and nanohybrid sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of PGr and the composite probe are demonstrated in Fig. 2 (A) and (B), respectively. The probe exhibited a rough surface and a particle size of about 190 nm. The functional groups of the synthesized composite probe and related materials were investigated using FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 2C) showed absorption peaks at 459 and 803 cm−1 which corresponded to SiO stretching and a strong absorption peak at 1053 cm−1 due to SiOSi stretching. A broad band at about 3378 cm−1 was ascribed to OH stretching. Characteristic peaks at 594 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to FeO stretching [27] and the band at 1621 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibration of adsorbed water [28]. The FT-IR spectrum of PGr (Fig. 2D) exhibited absorption peaks at 1273 and 1399 cm−1, corresponding to CO stretching and COH bending, respectively. The peak at 1578 cm−1 corresponded to the CC bond in graphene. Distinct peaks at 2927 and 2850 cm−1 corresponded to asymmetric and symmetric vibration of CH2 and a broad absorption band at 3424 cm−1 was due to OH group. In the spectrum of GQDs ( Fig. 2E), absorption peaks at 1132 and 1399 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching and COH bending, respectively. Absorption peaks at 1589 and 3449 cm−1 were due to CC and OH stretching. The FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized, nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 composite probe (Fig. 2F) presented absorption bands of Fe3O4@SiO2, PGr and GQDs. These results confirmed that the nanohybrid composite sensing probe was successfully fabricated. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe are shown in Fig. S1. The nanohybrid probe exhibited strong fluorescence emission intensity at 450 nm which implied that the probe had good optical properties which could be suitable for an ultra-sensitive optosensor. The quantum yields of GQDs and the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe were 0.85 and 0.64, respectively, calculated using Rhodamine 6G as a reference.

The saturation magnetization of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe was investigated using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM). The VSM curve showed that saturation magnetization was about 0.30 emu g−1 (Fig. S2) which enough to enable rapid separation of the magnetic nanoprobe from the solution by an external magnet.

The emission spectrum of the PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe during binding with levofloxacin exhibited low intensity due to fluorescence quenching by levofloxacin. When levofloxacin was eluted from the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe, fluorescence intensity recovered to the level exhibited by the non-imprinted nanoprobe (Fig. 3 A). A photograph of the nanohybrid sensing probe before and after elution of the template is shown in Fig. 3B. Fluorescence was weak in the presence of the template and the blue light emission was clearly visible after template removal. These results confirmed that the template molecule was completely eluted from the nanohybrid sensing probe and that the probe could be used to detect levofloxacin in real samples.

The effects of incubation time, ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker, the pH of the dispersion solution and the amount of PGr incorporated into the probe were investigated. The parameters were optimized to obtain the best sensitivity for the detection of levofloxacin. The fluorescence intensity change (F0/F) was used to indicate the optimal incubation time, while other parameters were optimized according to the sensitivity indicated by the slope of the calibration curve plotted between F0/F and concentrations of levofloxacin from 0.10 to 25 μg L-1.

To ensure good binding between levofloxacin and the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe, the effect of incubation time was studied from 0 to 30 min. The results indicated that quenching efficiency (F0/F) increased with binding time until 12 min and then remained constant (Fig. 4 A). Consequently, levofloxacin and the probe were incubated for 12 min before fluorescence was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication strategy of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe was performed using levofloxacin as the template, APTES as the monomer and TEOS as the cross-linker. The molar ratio of template to monomer and cross-linker are an important factor on specific recognition capability of the MIP sensing probe [29]. Therefore, to obtain the highest the sensitivity of nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe for the determination levofloxacin, the polymerization was optimized by varying the composition of the MIP as shown in Fig. 4B. The mole ratio of template to monomer of 1:8 provided the best sensitivity. At 1:6, sensitivity was lower since there were fewer functional groups to interact with the template and so fewer specific recognition sites were formed. In the reaction with template molecules (levofloxacin), a small amount of APTES did not provide enough functional groups (-NH2) to interact with target analyte via hydrogen bonding and produce the necessary number of specific binding sites on the MIP layer. The result was a nanocomposite probe of low sensitivity for the determination of target analyte. At a template to monomer ratio of 1:10, sensitivity was also lower because binding performance was reduced by excess monomer. The amount of cross-linker also affected the performance of the hybrid sensing probe. The template to cross-linker mole ratio of 1:70 provided the best sensitivity. At lower cross-linker ratios, the structure of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe was incomplete, resulting in a weak MIP layer. At higher cross-linker ratios, sensitivity was lower because excess cross-linker interrupted the interaction of monomer and template. Therefore, the template, monomer and cross-linker were combined at a ratio of 1:8:70 to fabricate the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe for levofloxacin detection.

The nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe was dispersed in citrate-phosphate buffer solution at pH 4.0, 5.0, 6.0 and 7.0 and the sensitivity for levofloxacin was determined. The sensitivity of the probe was highest when dispersed in the buffer at pH 6.0 (Fig. 4C). At pH lower than 6.0, hydrogen bonding between the probe and levofloxacin was disturbed by hydrogen ions in solution and sensitivity was low. Moreover, the sensitivity decreased at pH lower than 6.0 due to the –NH2 group of the APTES (functional monomer) in the recognition cavities is protonated at pH about 5.0 [30], which reduced hydrogen bonding between the binding site and levofloxacin. In addition, levofloxacin is also protonated at pH lower than 6.0 due to its pKa value is 6.02. The sensitivity decreased at higher pH because the silica layers of the probe could be ionized, resulting in structural obstruction of binding sites. Thus, dispersion in citrate-phosphate buffer at pH 6.0 was chosen as the optimum condition for levofloxacin detection.

The amount of PGr composited in the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe was investigated from 2.5 to 20 mg. As shown in Fig. 4D, the sensitivity of the probe increased with increasing loadings of PGr up to 10 mg. At PGr loadings below 10.0 mg, affinity binding was low between PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe and levofloxacin, and sensitivity was reduced. Sensitivity was highest at a PGr loading of 10.0 mg. At loadings above 10.0 mg, the structure of the PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe became too tight resulting in fewer recognition cavities. Hence, 10.0 mg of PGr was used as the optimum condition for the fabrication of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe.

Linearity, limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were investigated. The nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe was suspended in buffer solution at pH 6.0 containing different concentrations of levofloxacin. Fluorescence intensity was measured after incubation for 12 min. For comparison, the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe was also investigated in the same condition. The fluorescence emission of both probes decreased with increasing levofloxacin concentration (Fig. 5 A and B). The fluorescence intensity of the PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe was significantly more quenched than that of the PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe.

The quenching of fluorescence of the probe can be described by the Stern – Volmer equation [31] as follows: F0/F = 1+Ksv[C], where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of levofloxacin, respectively. Ksv and C are the fluorescence quenching constant and levofloxacin concentration, respectively. Usually, the quenching of QDs by organic molecules is either an energy transfer mechanism or an electron transfer mechanism [32]. In this work, since there was no spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of levofloxacin and the emission spectrum of the probe (Fig. S3), quenching by energy transfer was impossible [33]. Levofloxacin shows an apparent absorption band about 200−400 nm, which overlap with the excitation spectrum of the PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe as demonstrated in Fig. S4. So, the quenching mechanism was an electron transfer process that occurred when levofloxacin adsorbed and bound with recognition sites of the probe [34]. The electrons of GQDs can be excited from ground state to excited state, without template levofloxacin around the PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe, the excited electron of GQDs return to the valence band with producing fluorescence. While, in the presence of template (levofloxacin), the NH2 groups of the functional monomer (APTES) can interact with carbonyl of levofloxacin to form a Meisenheimer complex through hydrogen bonding [24]. The interaction would lead to electron transfer between GQDs and levofloxacin leading to fluorescence quenching.

The nanohybrid composite PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe exhibited a linear response to levofloxacin from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L-1 with a linear equation of y(F0/F) = (0.081 ± 0.003) X(levofloxacin) + (1.09 ± 0.04) and a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9951 (Fig. 5C). The LOD (3SD/Slope) and LOQ (10SD/Slope) were 0.03 and 0.10 μg L-1, respectively.

The nanohybrid sensing probe was also evaluated for levofloxacin detection at different stages of the probe’s fabrication. The probes tested included a GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2, a PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2, a GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 and a PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2. The sensitivity of the PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe was the highest (Fig. 5D) due to the presence of the affinity material and specific binding sites of MIP. These results confirmed that the combination of PGr and MIP improved the sensitivity of the probe toward levofloxacin.

The selectivity of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 probe was evaluated by comparing the sensitivity of the probe for the detection of levofloxacin and the structural analogs ciprofloxacin, lomefloxacin, marbofloxacin and sarafloxacin. The nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe exhibited high sensitivity toward levofloxacin and low sensitivity toward the analogs. The nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-NIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe exhibited similar sensitivity toward levofloxacin and the analogs. There was no significant difference in sensitivity because there were no specific recognition sites in the non-imprinted polymer (Fig. 6 A). Despite the fact that the structures and functional groups of levofloxacin and lomefloxacin were similar, raising the lomefloxacin concentration (Clomefloxacin/Clevofloxacin) in a competitive test had no significant effect on the sensitivity of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe (Fig. 6B).

The construction reproducibility of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing probe was evaluated by fabricating six different batches (n = 6) under the optimized conditions and the RSDs of average sensitivity were 1.9%. These results indicated good reproducibility (Fig. 6C). The stability of the nanohybrid sensing probe was evaluated in buffer solution at pH 6.0. The probe exhibited good stability. The response did not change significantly until 6 h (Fig. 6D), which is easily enough time for quantitative analysis by spectrofluorimetry.

The imprinting factor (IF) was also calculated to evaluate the specificity of the recognition sites. The ratio of Ksv of MIP and Ksv of NIP defines the specificity of imprinted polymers. The calculated IF of the nanohybrid PGr-GQDs-MIP-Fe3O4@SiO2 sensing was 4.26, showing the high specificity of the probe for levofloxacin, which can reduce matrix effects in real samples.

The nanohybrid optosensing probe was applied to detect levofloxacin in milk using matrix match calibration curve by spiking standard in milk samples. The analysis results are summarized in Table 1 . The accuracy of the developed optosensor was determined by detecting levofloxacin spiked in milk samples at different levels. Percent recovery was used to evaluate the accuracy of the system, which was calculated from the following equation: Recovery = (CTotal – CSample/CSpiked) × 100, where, CTotal is the levofloxacin concentration obtained in a spiked milk sample, CSample is the levofloxacin concentration in an original milk sample and CSpiked is the concentration of levofloxacin spiked in a milk sample. Recoveries between 91.8 and 100.5 % were achieved from spiked milk samples. The repeatability was performed in term of intra-day and inter-day precision, the RSDs between 1.7–5.0 % and 1.9–6.4 % for intra-day and inter-day, respectively. In addition, milk samples spiked with levofloxacin were also analyzed by HPLC and the results were compared with the results obtained with the developed optosensor. Chromatograms of levofloxacin in milk samples are shown in Fig. 7 A. Both methods produced similar results with an R2 of 0.9959 (Fig. 7B). These results confirmed the accuracy of the developed optosensor, which can therefore be utilized for ultra-trace detection of levofloxacin. Compared to previous methods, the developed optosensor has a lower limit of detection, and comparable or better recoveries and precision (Table 2 ). Other advantages of the optosensor are its simple procedure, rapid detection and convenience.

A nanohybrid magnetic sensing probe was fabricated by integrating porous graphene, graphene quantum dots, and magnetite nanoparticles. Combining the properties of GQDs and MIP, the nanohybrid sensing probe was the basis of a sensitive and selective optosensor for levofloxacin detection. The integrated porous graphene improved the mass transfer of levofloxacin to recognition sites to produce a fast response to levofloxacin. The incorporated magnetite nanoparticles enriched levofloxacin in real samples which enabled analyte detection at ultra-trace levels. The developed nanohybrid sensing probe showed good accuracy (recoveries 91.5–100.5 %). The method made use of simple, low-cost equipment, the procedure was rapid and convenient and sample volumes were small. The developed strategy can be applied for the fabrication of sensing probes for other target compounds by changing the template molecule.

Nutnicha Chansud: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Noppanut Longnapa: Formal analysis, Methodology. Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT5-RSA63022-03), the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2021.114316.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel electrochemical sensor based on poly(p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid)-reduced graphene oxide composite film for the sensitive and selective detection of levofloxacin in human urine
J. Electroanal. Chem.
